itineraryStops.name: Hyeopjae Beach
itineraryStops.category: West-coast emerald beach + Biyangdo Island view
itineraryStops.description:
**Hyeopjae Beach (협재 해수욕장)** is one of **Jeju's Top 3 west-coast beaches** and broadly ranked among the island's overall **Top 10 beaches**, defined by its **distinctive silvery-white sand** — formed from **crushed seashells + coral fragments mixed with fine sand**, producing a luminescence rare on the Korean Peninsula. 

The beach forms a **9 km shoreline** when paired with its **twin-beach companion Geumneung Beach (immediately south, walkable along the same shore, less crowded)** — the two-beach pairing is one of the route's signature photographic periods. 

The signature view is **Biyangdo Island (비양도)** — a **1.2-km offshore volcanic island with ~200 permanent residents**, accessible by **20-minute ferry from Hallim Port**. Biyangdo is undergoing **Korea's first carbon-free island certification process**, an environmental policy showcase. 

The beach's **emerald-turquoise water** comes from the shallow coral-sand seabed, and at **low tide, tidal pools reveal hermit crabs, small shrimp, and juvenile fish** — Biyangdo can appear 'walkable' across the shallow expanse. 

**Pine forest backdrop (Songlim)** behind the beach offers free camping ground and shaded walking paths. Behind the dunes runs Jeju's most-developed **west-coast café strip** — concentrated cafe + restaurant + boutique architecture catering to domestic tourism. 

**Sunset behind Biyangdo over emerald water** is the visual signature and the route's strongest west-coast sunset framing. **FREE admission**; **lifeguarded swim period July 1 – August 31**; ramp-style **wheelchair access** at multiple beach points; public restrooms + changing rooms + showers + equipment rentals year-round. 

Adjacent **Hallim Park** (botanical garden + lava caves + folk village, separate ₩10,000 admission, 1.5 km away) is occasionally substituted in winter.
highlights: **Top 3 west-coast Jeju beaches / Top 10 overall** — emerald-turquoise water + silvery sand
**Distinctive silvery-white sand** — crushed seashells + coral fragments
**9 km shoreline** when paired with twin-beach **Geumneung Beach** (less crowded, walkable)
**Biyangdo Island** — 1.2 km offshore, ~200 residents, **Korea's first carbon-free island certification**
**Sunset behind Biyangdo** — one of Jeju's classic west-coast sunset scenes
**Pine forest (Songlim) backdrop** with free camping ground
**Tidal pools at low tide** — hermit crabs, shrimp, juvenile fish; Biyangdo 'walkable' optical effect
**Lifeguarded swim period July 1 – August 31**; wheelchair-accessible ramp at multiple points
**Most-developed west-coast café strip** behind the beach
timeUsed: Main beach walk + photo periods — ~20 min
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **West-coast beach opener and natural lead-in to Hallim Park.** Hyeopjae sits very close to Hallim Park, so pairing the beach and garden in the same morning keeps driving low and gives the day an easy visual start. The morning timing is also better for the beach: the water color is usually clearer, the sand is less crowded, and Biyangdo gives the first wide landscape photo before the tour moves into gardens, cliffs, waterfalls, and tea fields.

sources: VisitJeju — Hyeopjae Beach + Biyangdo Island official listings
Jeju Special Self-Governing Province — Top Jeju beaches ranking + amenity registry
Korea Maritime + Ports Office — Hallim Port ferry schedule to Biyangdo
Jeju Tourism Organization — west-coast sunset photography spots registry
Hallim-eup local cultural office — Geumneung twin-beach pairing documentation

smartNotes.facilities: Multiple cafés along beachfront; convenience stores
smartNotes.tip: Combine with Hallim Park (5 min walk); avoid summer weekends for fewer crowds

itineraryStops.name: Hallim Park (Hydrangea Festival)
itineraryStops.category: Hydrangea highlight + 9 themed gardens + 2 lava-tube caves
itineraryStops.description:
**Hallim Park (한림공원, founded 1971)** is one of the southwest coast's major botanical parks — a **~99,000 m² (originally 100,000 pyeong / 330,580 sqm) garden complex** that **founder Song Bong-gyu transformed from a sandy wasteland into a green paradise** by importing soil and planting palm seeds across a half-century. 

The park draws **~1.2 million visitors annually** and organizes its space into **nine themed gardens**: the **Subtropical Botanic Garden**, the **Palm Tree Avenue**, the **Wild Grass and Flower Garden**, the **Hyeopjaegul + Ssangyonggul Caves**, the **Jeju Stone and Bonsai Garden**, the **Jae-Am Folk Village** (Joseon-era stone-walled hanok with on-site Dolharubang Restaurant), the **Bird Garden**, the **Jeju Stone Exhibit Hall**, and the **Water Garden**. 

Hallim's defining structural decision is its **monthly festival calendar** — **January Daffodil → February Plum Flower → March Tulip → April Cherry Blossom → May Wild Flower / Bougainvillea → **June Hydrangea** → July–September Lotus + Water Lily → October Cosmos / Pink Muhly → November Chrysanthemum → December Camellia** — a 12-month cycle that usually offers a **named seasonal flower theme throughout the year**. 

The park's two geological landmarks are **Hyeopjaegul Cave (~200m × 10m × 5m, ~25 million years old)** and **Ssangyonggul Cave (~400m × 6m × 3m, 'Two Dragons')** — both part of the same **17 km lava-tube system** but uniquely classified as **'lava-tube and limestone cave features'** because lava-tube walls were later coated with **limestone (calcium carbonate) leached from shells in the soil above**, producing the rare global combination of **lava-tube morphology + limestone stalactites/stalagmites** in a single cave area. 

Cave temperature holds at **17–18°C year-round**, making them natural summer refuges. The park's guest list reflects its diplomatic stature: former Chinese President Jiang Zemin, former Japanese Prime Minister Nakasone Yasuhiro, former Korean President Kim Dae-jung, former Samsung Chairman Lee Kun-hee, and former Hyundai Group Chairman Chung Ju-yung have all visited.
highlights: **Founded 1971** by Song Bong-gyu — sandy wasteland transformed into a green paradise
**~99,000 m² / 80 acres** — 9 themed gardens + ~1.2 million visitors per year
**June Hydrangea Festival** — one of the park's signature early-summer draws
**Monthly festival calendar** — named active festival every month of the year
**Hyeopjaegul Cave** (~200m × 10m × 5m, ~25 million years old) — lava-tube + limestone stalactites
**Ssangyonggul Cave** ('Two Dragons', ~400m × 6m × 3m) — branching cave paths
**Lava-tube and limestone cave features** — a distinctive cave experience inside the park
Cave temp **17–18°C year-round** — natural summer refuge
Diplomatic guest list: **Jiang Zemin, Nakasone, Kim Dae-jung, Lee Kun-hee, Chung Ju-yung**
timeUsed: Subtropical / Palm / Wild Flower gardens — 30 min
Hydrangea garden + Jae-Am Folk Village — 35 min
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **The day's first major hydrangea highlight and the stop that defines the southwest route.** Hallim Park works well in the late morning because it combines flower gardens, shade, caves, and plenty of walking space in one place. In June, the hydrangea festival gives the route its main flower color, while the Hyeopjaegul and Ssangyonggul caves offer a cooler break if the weather is hot or rainy. The longer 120-minute stay gives guests enough time for the gardens, cave paths, and photos without making the stop feel rushed.

itineraryStops.name: Lunch (Southwest Jeju)
itineraryStops.description:
**Lunch is at a local Jeju-style restaurant in the Hallim / Aewol-tail / Andeok corridor**, selected from a vetted shortlist of family-run establishments that the operator has approved for **English-friendly menus, dietary accommodation (vegetarian + halal-friendly chicken/seafood options on request), and west-coast Jeju specialties** at fair group pricing. 

Typical lunch options include **galchi-jorim (braised silver hairtail)**, **heuk-dwaeji bulgogi (Jeju black pork bulgogi)**, **mom-guk (Jeju seaweed-pork soup)**, and **bibimbap with seasonal namul**. 

**Mom-guk in particular is a southwest-Jeju regional specialty** that doesn't appear on east-coast tours. Like the east-route lunch, the exact restaurant is selected the day before based on group size, dietary requests, and kitchen availability — guests are notified at the morning briefing. 

**Lunch cost is at guests' own expense** (~₩12,000–₩18,000 per person) and is **not included** in the tour fee. Beverages and rice/banchan refills are always included with the meal.
highlights: **Southwest-Jeju local restaurant** — Hallim / Aewol-tail / Andeok corridor
Vetted shortlist: **English-friendly menus** + dietary accommodation
Signature options: **galchi-jorim, heuk-dwaeji bulgogi, mom-guk (regional), bibimbap**
**Mom-guk** = west-Jeju regional specialty (seaweed-pork soup) not on east tours
**Lunch at guests' own expense** (~₩12,000–₩18,000/person, not included)
Vegetarian + halal-friendly options on request
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **Comfortable midday break.** Lunch sits after the longer Hyeopjae and Hallim Park morning and before the Jungmun cliff-and-waterfall section. The 60-minute meal also gives the guide a flexible time buffer if Hallim Park takes slightly longer during the busy flower season.

smartNotes.tip: Vegetarian options on request — notify the day before.

visitBasics.admission: Lunch cost paid direct, ≈10,000–15,000 KRW typical (Korean local restaurants near route)

itineraryStops.name: Jusangjeolli Cliff & Cheonjeyeon Falls
itineraryStops.category: Compound stop — Natural Monument 443 columnar joints + Natural Monument 378 three-tier waterfall
itineraryStops.description:
**This is a paired stop** combining two of Jungmun's signature Natural Monuments into a single ~75-min walking arc — the locally practical southwest treatment for two highlights that sit a **7-min drive (~3 km)** apart. Stop A is **Daepo Jusangjeolli Cliff (대포 주상절리, 'Daepo Columnar Joints')** — designated **Natural Monument No. 443 on January 6, 2005** (the most recent Jeju coastal Natural Monument designation), a **1.75 km coastal cliff face of polygonal black basalt columns** formed when **Hallasan-eruption lava cooled rapidly against Pacific seawater ~140,000–250,000 years ago**, fracturing the basalt into **hexagonal and pentagonal columns up to 30–40 m tall**. 

The site is part of the **Jeju Volcanic Island and Lava Tubes UNESCO Global Geopark (2010)**. Wave heights during peak south swells reach **20+ meters** crashing against the column base, producing the iconic Jeju basalt-cliff seascape. Stop B is **Cheonjeyeon Falls (천제연, 'Pond of the Emperor of Heaven')** — designated **Natural Monument No. 378 (1993)** for its rare ecological combination of a three-tier waterfall + dense subtropical-warm-temperate transition forest. 

The name traces to a **Joseon-era Daoist legend that seven heavenly nymphs (선녀, *seonnyeo*) descended nightly to bathe in the upper pond**. 

The site's signature visual is **Seonimgyo Bridge (선임교, 'Seven Nymph Bridge')** — a 128-m-long arched footbridge with seven nymph statues facing the upper falls. 

The **reason this pairing works**: visiting both as a single 75-min stop preserves the day's Hallim+Camellia+Osulloc floral pacing without splitting it across two transits, and the **Jusangjeolli cliff geology + Cheonjeyeon waterfall+forest layering** gives the southwest route its clear **non-floral counterpoint** to the day's three garden highlights.
highlights: **Compound stop** — 2 Natural Monuments paired in one 75-min walk (3 km / 7-min drive between)
**Daepo Jusangjeolli — Natural Monument No. 443 (2005)** — most recent Jeju coastal NM
**1.75 km coastal cliff** of hexagonal/pentagonal black basalt columns up to 30–40m tall
Part of **Jeju UNESCO Global Geopark (2010)** — formed ~140,000–250,000 years ago
**Wave heights up to 20m+** during peak south swells crashing against columns
**Cheonjeyeon Falls — Natural Monument No. 378 (1993)** — three-tier waterfall + transition forest
**Seven Nymphs (선녀) Joseon Daoist legend** — heavenly nymphs descended to bathe nightly
**Seonimgyo Bridge (선임교)** — 128-m arched footbridge with 7 nymph statues
**Floral counterpoint** — the day's only non-garden geological + waterfall stop
timeUsed: Jusangjeolli cliff observation deck + photos — 25 min
Drive to Cheonjeyeon (~7 min) + restroom — 12 min
Cheonjeyeon Seonimgyo Bridge + upper-pond viewpoint — 25 min
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **The day's geological and waterfall counterpoint to the flower gardens.** Jusangjeolli and Cheonjeyeon sit close together in Jungmun, so visiting them as a pair gives the day strong variety without adding too much extra driving. The basalt cliff, ocean spray, forest path, and waterfall scenery keep the route from becoming only a flower-garden day, while the shared stop still leaves enough time for Hallim Park, Camellia Hill, and Osulloc.

itineraryStops.name: Camellia Hill (Hydrangea Tunnel)
itineraryStops.category: Hydrangea tunnel + 172,000 m² largest camellia arboretum in the East
itineraryStops.description:
**Camellia Hill (카멜리아힐, Jeju Camellia Arboretum)** is a **172,000 m² botanical garden** in Andeok, **the largest camellia arboretum in the East** by acreage and species count: **6,000 camellia trees representing over 500 species sourced from 80 countries**, accumulated over **30+ years** of curation by founder Yang Jong-mun. 

The garden's signature is its **year-round flowering calendar**, deliberately engineered so that visiting in any season produces a different color palette: **November–March = camellia peak** (red, pink, white, cream-beige varieties — the garden's namesake season); **March–May = cherry blossoms + tulips + late camellias** layered together in dense color mass; **June–August = hydrangeas** (both outdoor stands and a climate-controlled greenhouse showing year-round display); **September–October = fall foliage + late-season bloomers**. 

Walking paths thread through themed sections — **European Garden, Korean Native Forest Section, Cherry Blossom Walk, Hydrangea Field** — connected by a winding 2.5-km loop that takes ~60–75 min to complete at a leisurely pace. Notable architectural moment: a **glass greenhouse + gallery + cafe** complex at the entrance, which serves as both indoor weather refuge (the only ~15% indoor portion of the visit) and a tea/coffee dwell space with garden views. 

**Drama filming credit**: variety show **'EXchange / Transit Love 3' (환승연애)** featured Camellia Hill as the location of Ju-won and Yu-jeong's memorable date scene — a draw for fans of the K-reality genre. 

**Open 365 days a year**; **no outdoor lighting** means the garden closes at sundown (winter ~17:00, summer ~19:00). Admission: **Adult ₩12,000 / Youth + Senior ₩10,000 / Child (4–11) ₩9,000 / FREE under 3**.
highlights: **172,000 m²** — largest camellia arboretum in the East
**6,000 trees / 500+ species from 80 countries** — accumulated 30+ years
**Year-round bloom calendar** — Nov–Mar camellias, Mar–May cherry + tulip, Jun–Aug hydrangea
**Themed sections**: European Garden, Korean Native Forest, Cherry Walk, Hydrangea Field
Glass **greenhouse + gallery + cafe** complex — indoor refuge (15% of visit)
**'EXchange / Transit Love 3'** filming location — Ju-won & Yu-jeong's date scene
**Open 365 days**; closes at sundown (no outdoor lighting)
Admission **₩12,000 adult / ₩10,000 youth+senior / ₩9,000 child (4–11) / FREE under 3**
**~85% outdoor** garden walking — comfortable shoes recommended
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **The afternoon hydrangea tunnel and main photo stop.** Camellia Hill gives the tour a softer garden experience after Hallim Park and Jungmun. The hydrangea tunnel and covered walkways are easy to enjoy at a relaxed pace, and the later timing often works well for warmer light on the pink, blue, and purple flowers. Together, Hallim Park and Camellia Hill create two different hydrangea moods: a larger botanical park in the morning and a gentler photo garden in the afternoon.

sources: Korea Tourism Organization — Top Korean botanical gardens registry
Kakao TV / Wavve programming archive — 'EXchange / Transit Love 3' filming locations

itineraryStops.name: Osulloc Tea Museum & Tea Fields
itineraryStops.category: Amorepacific tea-culture flagship + green-tea fields finale
itineraryStops.description:
**O'Sulloc Tea Museum (오설록 티 뮤지엄, '오설록' meaning 'meeting snow + green')** is the tea-culture flagship of **Amorepacific Corporation** — Korea's largest beauty company. Founder **Suh Sung-whan personally pioneered the project in 1979**, reclaiming volcanic ash farmland on Jeju's Seogwang plateau when conventional wisdom held tea couldn't be grown commercially in Korea. 

Today the **Seogwang Dawon plantation (서광다원)** is **Korea's largest green-tea field**, producing **~700 tonnes annually** across Amorepacific's three Jeju estates. 

The museum building **opened 2001** in the plantation's heart and was named to **designboom's Top 10 art museums** for its minimalist green-roofed pavilion-amid-tea-rows design. 

**FREE admission**, daily **09:00–18:00**, ~**2 million annual visitors**. The visit splits naturally into three segments: **(1) Museum interior** — tea production process, antique tea-ware collection from Tang dynasty (618–907 CE) onward, and rooftop observation deck with panoramic plantation view; **(2) Tea House cafe** — signature **green tea rolling cake (오설록 롤케이크)** and **green tea ice cream** served from the tea-leaf harvest year directly; **(3) Plantation walking paths** — gravel rows between tea bushes, popular for sunset photography at the western edge. 

**Innisfree Jeju House** (Amorepacific's natural cosmetics brand) is a **5-minute walk** for guests interested in the broader brand ecosystem — also free admission with optional tangerine soap DIY workshop (₩15,000). 

The museum building itself, designed by Korean architect **Mass Studies (Cho Min-suk)**, takes a deliberately low-profile horizontal form that mirrors the tea-row geometry rather than competing with the agricultural landscape — the architectural deference earning it both the designboom Top 10 listing and ongoing use as a case study in Korean contemporary museum design. 

Optional add-on: the **Osulloc Tea Class (₩30,000 per person, 60 min, advance booking required)** — guided tea-flight tasting with brewing technique demonstration on Korean ceramics, available daily **10:00–16:00**, English-speaking instruction on Wed/Sat sessions.
highlights: **Amorepacific** flagship — Korea's largest beauty company; founder Suh Sung-whan pioneered Jeju tea **1979**
**Seogwang Dawon = Korea's largest green-tea field**; ~700 tonnes annual production
Museum **opened 2001** — designboom Top 10 art museums (architecture)
**FREE admission**, daily **09:00–18:00**, ~2 million annual visitors
Antique tea-ware collection spanning **Tang dynasty (618–907 CE)** to modern Korean Joseon ceramics
**Green tea rolling cake** + **green tea ice cream** = signature cafe items
**Innisfree Jeju House** 5-min walk — free, optional tangerine soap DIY ₩15,000
Plantation walking paths **best at golden hour (16:00–17:00)** for photography
Rooftop deck + ground-level rows give two distinct elevations for tea-row composition
timeUsed: Tea House cafe (rolling cake + ice cream) — ~20 min
Optional Innisfree Jeju House visit — ~10 min
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **Tea-culture finale and calm non-floral close.** Osulloc gives the day a clean finish after the beach, flower gardens, cliffs, and waterfall. The green tea fields add a different color and mood to the photo set, while the museum, cafe, restrooms, and optional tea purchases make it a comfortable final stop before the drive back to Jeju City. It also adds a recognizable Korean tea-culture story rather than ending the day with another flower garden.

sources: designboom Top 10 art museums archival listing
Korea Tea Industry Association — production statistics

items.label: Hydrangea Season Late May - mid-July

staticQuestions.question: Is this good for first-time visitors to Jeju?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes, if you're visiting during hydrangea season (late May to mid-July, often strongest around June). Outside that season, our West & South year-round route covers the same region with year-round POIs.

staticQuestions.question: When exactly is hydrangea season?
staticQuestions.answer: Approximately late May to mid-July (peak mid-June), with peak typically in mid-June. We update the booking page with year-specific forecast in early May.

staticQuestions.question: What if it rains?
staticQuestions.answer: Light rain — we operate when conditions are safe, and hydrangeas can photograph beautifully in soft rain. Heavy rain — we offer free rescheduling during the tour season when possible.

staticQuestions.question: Why two hydrangea stops?
staticQuestions.answer: Hallim Park is an open festival garden; Camellia Hill is a tunnel format. Same flower, complementary compositions.

staticQuestions.question: Is this suitable for children?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes, age 6+. Walking is moderate at most stops; hydrangea gardens are family-friendly.

staticQuestions.question: Is this ok for seniors?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes, mostly easy walking. Cheonjeyeon waterfall path has some stairs — can be skipped while group views Jusangjeolli.

staticQuestions.question: Where do you pick up?
staticQuestions.answer: Hotel lobby pickup within Jeju City is free. Pickup from Seogwipo, Aewol, or Seongsan adds 70,000 KRW due to extra driving distance.

staticQuestions.question: Is lunch included?
staticQuestions.answer: Lunch cost is separate. Expect 12,000-18,000 KRW per person.

staticQuestions.question: Are admission fees included?
staticQuestions.answer: Admissions are not bundled by default. Budget ≈30,000 KRW per person: Hallim Park 15,000, Camellia Hill 10,000, Jusangjeolli 2,000, Cheonjeyeon 2,500, Osulloc free.

staticQuestions.question: Is Osulloc included?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — museum entry is free. The tea house is separate (optional purchase).

staticQuestions.question: What's the difference vs our West & South year-round route?
staticQuestions.answer: Different POIs and seasonality. Our West & South year-round route is year-round and visits Hallasan 1100, Osulloc, and Aewol. This tour is summer-only and adds Hyeopjae, Hallim, Jusangjeolli, Cheonjeyeon, Camellia Hill while keeping Osulloc as the closing stop.

Designed for **June peak hydrangea window (June 10–25)** with Hallim caves + Camellia Hall + Osulloc museum as the 3-tier indoor weather-alternative stack. **Catalog-novel pattern**: Jusangjeolli+Cheonjeyeon compound stop is the first of its kind in the catalog. Cross-batch reuse: 3 of 5 substantive bodies imported verbatim (15a T2 Hyeopjae, 15a T1 Camellia Hill, 14a T2 Osulloc); Hallim Park authored fresh + compound stop authored fresh.

itineraryStops.name: Hyeopjae Beach
itineraryStops.category: West-coast emerald beach + Biyangdo Island view
itineraryStops.description: **Hyeopjae Beach (협재 해수욕장)** is one of **Jeju's Top 3 west-coast beaches** and broadly ranked among the island's overall **Top 10 beaches**, defined by its **distinctive silvery-white sand** — formed from **crushed seashells + coral fragments mixed with fine sand**, producing a luminescence rare on the Korean Peninsula. The beach forms a **9 km shoreline** when paired with its **twin-beach companion Geumneung Beach (immediately south, walkable along the same shore, less crowded)** — the two-beach pairing is one of the route's signature photographic periods. The signature view is **Biyangdo Island (비양도)** — a **1.2-km offshore volcanic island with ~200 permanent residents**, accessible by **20-minute ferry from Hallim Port**. Biyangdo is undergoing **Korea's first carbon-free island certification process**, an environmental policy showcase. The beach's **emerald-turquoise water** comes from the shallow coral-sand seabed, and at **low tide, tidal pools reveal hermit crabs, small shrimp, and juvenile fish** — Biyangdo can appear 'walkable' across the shallow expanse. **Pine forest backdrop (Songlim)** behind the beach offers free camping ground and shaded walking paths. Behind the dunes runs Jeju's most-developed **west-coast café strip** — concentrated cafe + restaurant + boutique architecture catering to domestic tourism. **Sunset behind Biyangdo over emerald water** is the visual signature and the route's strongest west-coast sunset framing. **FREE admission**; **lifeguarded swim period July 1 – August 31**; ramp-style **wheelchair access** at multiple beach points; public restrooms + changing rooms + showers + equipment rentals year-round. Adjacent **Hallim Park** (botanical garden + lava caves + folk village, separate ₩10,000 admission, 1.5 km away) is occasionally substituted in winter.
highlights: Emerald turquoise water
View of Biyangdo island
White sand walking promenade
timeUsed: Brief context on west coast geology
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **West-coast beach opener and natural lead-in to Hallim Park.** Hyeopjae sits very close to Hallim Park, so pairing the beach and garden in the same morning keeps driving low and gives the day an easy visual start. The morning timing is also better for the beach: the water color is usually clearer, the sand is less crowded, and Biyangdo gives the first wide landscape photo before the tour moves into gardens, cliffs, waterfalls, and tea fields.

visitBasics.hours: Open 24 hours; managed swim season July–August

smartNotes.photo: Sunset shots with Biyangdo silhouette; turquoise color is most vivid at midday under direct sun
smartNotes.facilities: Multiple cafés along beachfront; convenience stores
smartNotes.tip: Combine with Hallim Park (5 min walk); avoid summer weekends for fewer crowds

itineraryStops.name: Hallim Park (Hydrangea Festival)
itineraryStops.category: Hydrangea highlight + 9 themed gardens + 2 lava-tube caves
itineraryStops.description: **Hallim Park (한림공원, founded 1971)** is one of the southwest coast's major botanical parks — a **~99,000 m² (originally 100,000 pyeong / 330,580 sqm) garden complex** that **founder Song Bong-gyu transformed from a sandy wasteland into a green paradise** by importing soil and planting palm seeds across a half-century. The park draws **~1.2 million visitors annually** and organizes its space into **nine themed gardens**: the **Subtropical Botanic Garden**, the **Palm Tree Avenue**, the **Wild Grass and Flower Garden**, the **Hyeopjaegul + Ssangyonggul Caves**, the **Jeju Stone and Bonsai Garden**, the **Jae-Am Folk Village** (Joseon-era stone-walled hanok with on-site Dolharubang Restaurant), the **Bird Garden**, the **Jeju Stone Exhibit Hall**, and the **Water Garden**. Hallim's defining structural decision is its **monthly festival calendar** — **January Daffodil → February Plum Flower → March Tulip → April Cherry Blossom → May Wild Flower / Bougainvillea → **June Hydrangea** → July–September Lotus + Water Lily → October Cosmos / Pink Muhly → November Chrysanthemum → December Camellia** — a 12-month cycle that usually offers a **named seasonal flower theme throughout the year**. The park's two geological landmarks are **Hyeopjaegul Cave (~200m × 10m × 5m, ~25 million years old)** and **Ssangyonggul Cave (~400m × 6m × 3m, 'Two Dragons')** — both part of the same **17 km lava-tube system** but uniquely classified as **'lava-tube and limestone cave features'** because lava-tube walls were later coated with **limestone (calcium carbonate) leached from shells in the soil above**, producing the rare global combination of **lava-tube morphology + limestone stalactites/stalagmites** in a single cave area. Cave temperature holds at **17–18°C year-round**, making them natural summer refuges. The park's guest list reflects its diplomatic stature: former Chinese President Jiang Zemin, former Japanese Prime Minister Nakasone Yasuhiro, former Korean President Kim Dae-jung, former Samsung Chairman Lee Kun-hee, and former Hyundai Group Chairman Chung Ju-yung have all visited.

itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **The day's first major hydrangea highlight and the stop that defines the southwest route.** Hallim Park works well in the late morning because it combines flower gardens, shade, caves, and plenty of walking space in one place. In June, the hydrangea festival gives the route its main flower color, while the Hyeopjaegul and Ssangyonggul caves offer a cooler break if the weather is hot or rainy. The longer 120-minute stay gives guests enough time for the gardens, cave paths, and photos without making the stop feel rushed.

itineraryStops.name: Lunch (Southwest Jeju)
itineraryStops.description: **Lunch is at a local Jeju-style restaurant in the Hallim / Aewol-tail / Andeok corridor**, selected from a vetted shortlist of family-run establishments that the operator has approved for **English-friendly menus, dietary accommodation (vegetarian + halal-friendly chicken/seafood options on request), and west-coast Jeju specialties** at fair group pricing. Typical lunch options include **galchi-jorim (braised silver hairtail)**, **heuk-dwaeji bulgogi (Jeju black pork bulgogi)**, **mom-guk (Jeju seaweed-pork soup)**, and **bibimbap with seasonal namul**. **Mom-guk in particular is a southwest-Jeju regional specialty** that doesn't appear on east-coast tours. Like the east-route lunch, the exact restaurant is selected the day before based on group size, dietary requests, and kitchen availability — guests are notified at the morning briefing. **Lunch cost is at guests' own expense** (~₩12,000–₩18,000 per person) and is **not included** in the tour fee. Beverages and rice/banchan refills are always included with the meal.

itineraryStops.name: Jusangjeolli Cliff & Cheonjeyeon Falls
itineraryStops.category: Compound stop — Natural Monument 443 columnar joints + Natural Monument 378 three-tier waterfall
itineraryStops.description: **This is a paired stop** combining two of Jungmun's signature Natural Monuments into a single ~75-min walking arc — the locally practical southwest treatment for two highlights that sit a **7-min drive (~3 km)** apart. Stop A is **Daepo Jusangjeolli Cliff (대포 주상절리, 'Daepo Columnar Joints')** — designated **Natural Monument No. 443 on January 6, 2005** (the most recent Jeju coastal Natural Monument designation), a **1.75 km coastal cliff face of polygonal black basalt columns** formed when **Hallasan-eruption lava cooled rapidly against Pacific seawater ~140,000–250,000 years ago**, fracturing the basalt into **hexagonal and pentagonal columns up to 30–40 m tall**. The site is part of the **Jeju Volcanic Island and Lava Tubes UNESCO Global Geopark (2010)**. Wave heights during peak south swells reach **20+ meters** crashing against the column base, producing the iconic Jeju basalt-cliff seascape. Stop B is **Cheonjeyeon Falls (천제연, 'Pond of the Emperor of Heaven')** — designated **Natural Monument No. 378 (1993)** for its rare ecological combination of a three-tier waterfall + dense subtropical-warm-temperate transition forest. The name traces to a **Joseon-era Daoist legend that seven heavenly nymphs (선녀, *seonnyeo*) descended nightly to bathe in the upper pond**. The site's signature visual is **Seonimgyo Bridge (선임교, 'Seven Nymph Bridge')** — a 128-m-long arched footbridge with seven nymph statues facing the upper falls. The **reason this pairing works**: visiting both as a single 75-min stop preserves the day's Hallim+Camellia+Osulloc floral pacing without splitting it across two transits, and the **Jusangjeolli cliff geology + Cheonjeyeon waterfall+forest layering** gives the southwest route its clear **non-floral counterpoint** to the day's three garden highlights.
highlights: Hexagonal columnar basalt cliff
Three-tier waterfall in subtropical forest
UNESCO Geopark + Biosphere
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **The day's geological and waterfall counterpoint to the flower gardens.** Jusangjeolli and Cheonjeyeon sit close together in Jungmun, so visiting them as a pair gives the day strong variety without adding too much extra driving. The basalt cliff, ocean spray, forest path, and waterfall scenery keep the route from becoming only a flower-garden day, while the shared stop still leaves enough time for Hallim Park, Camellia Hill, and Osulloc.

visitBasics.admission: Jusangjeolli: Adult 2,000 / Child 1,000. Cheonjeyeon: Adult 2,500 / Child 1,350. Combined ≈4,500 KRW per adult.

itineraryStops.name: Camellia Hill (Hydrangea Tunnel)
itineraryStops.category: Hydrangea tunnel + 172,000 m² largest camellia arboretum in the East
itineraryStops.description: **Camellia Hill (카멜리아힐, Jeju Camellia Arboretum)** is a **172,000 m² botanical garden** in Andeok, **the largest camellia arboretum in the East** by acreage and species count: **6,000 camellia trees representing over 500 species sourced from 80 countries**, accumulated over **30+ years** of curation by founder Yang Jong-mun. The garden's signature is its **year-round flowering calendar**, deliberately engineered so that visiting in any season produces a different color palette: **November–March = camellia peak** (red, pink, white, cream-beige varieties — the garden's namesake season); **March–May = cherry blossoms + tulips + late camellias** layered together in dense color mass; **June–August = hydrangeas** (both outdoor stands and a climate-controlled greenhouse showing year-round display); **September–October = fall foliage + late-season bloomers**. Walking paths thread through themed sections — **European Garden, Korean Native Forest Section, Cherry Blossom Walk, Hydrangea Field** — connected by a winding 2.5-km loop that takes ~60–75 min to complete at a leisurely pace. Notable architectural moment: a **glass greenhouse + gallery + cafe** complex at the entrance, which serves as both indoor weather refuge (the only ~15% indoor portion of the visit) and a tea/coffee dwell space with garden views. **Drama filming credit**: variety show **'EXchange / Transit Love 3' (환승연애)** featured Camellia Hill as the location of Ju-won and Yu-jeong's memorable date scene — a draw for fans of the K-reality genre. **Open 365 days a year**; **no outdoor lighting** means the garden closes at sundown (winter ~17:00, summer ~19:00). Admission: **Adult ₩12,000 / Youth + Senior ₩10,000 / Child (4–11) ₩9,000 / FREE under 3**.
highlights: Hydrangea tunnel walk

itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **The afternoon hydrangea tunnel and main photo stop.** Camellia Hill gives the tour a softer garden experience after Hallim Park and Jungmun. The hydrangea tunnel and covered walkways are easy to enjoy at a relaxed pace, and the later timing often works well for warmer light on the pink, blue, and purple flowers. Together, Hallim Park and Camellia Hill create two different hydrangea moods: a larger botanical park in the morning and a gentler photo garden in the afternoon.

itineraryStops.name: Osulloc Tea Museum & Tea Fields
itineraryStops.category: Amorepacific tea-culture flagship + green-tea fields finale
itineraryStops.description: **O'Sulloc Tea Museum (오설록 티 뮤지엄, '오설록' meaning 'meeting snow + green')** is the tea-culture flagship of **Amorepacific Corporation** — Korea's largest beauty company. Founder **Suh Sung-whan personally pioneered the project in 1979**, reclaiming volcanic ash farmland on Jeju's Seogwang plateau when conventional wisdom held tea couldn't be grown commercially in Korea. Today the **Seogwang Dawon plantation (서광다원)** is **Korea's largest green-tea field**, producing **~700 tonnes annually** across Amorepacific's three Jeju estates. The museum building **opened 2001** in the plantation's heart and was named to **designboom's Top 10 art museums** for its minimalist green-roofed pavilion-amid-tea-rows design. **FREE admission**, daily **09:00–18:00**, ~**2 million annual visitors**. The visit splits naturally into three segments: **(1) Museum interior** — tea production process, antique tea-ware collection from Tang dynasty (618–907 CE) onward, and rooftop observation deck with panoramic plantation view; **(2) Tea House cafe** — signature **green tea rolling cake (오설록 롤케이크)** and **green tea ice cream** served from the tea-leaf harvest year directly; **(3) Plantation walking paths** — gravel rows between tea bushes, popular for sunset photography at the western edge. **Innisfree Jeju House** (Amorepacific's natural cosmetics brand) is a **5-minute walk** for guests interested in the broader brand ecosystem — also free admission with optional tangerine soap DIY workshop (₩15,000). The museum building itself, designed by Korean architect **Mass Studies (Cho Min-suk)**, takes a deliberately low-profile horizontal form that mirrors the tea-row geometry rather than competing with the agricultural landscape — the architectural deference earning it both the designboom Top 10 listing and ongoing use as a case study in Korean contemporary museum design. Optional add-on: the **Osulloc Tea Class (₩30,000 per person, 60 min, advance booking required)** — guided tea-flight tasting with brewing technique demonstration on Korean ceramics, available daily **10:00–16:00**, English-speaking instruction on Wed/Sat sessions.
highlights: Vast green tea field photo zones
Tea museum exhibits
Green tea ice cream and snacks at tea house
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **Tea-culture finale and calm non-floral close.** Osulloc gives the day a clean finish after the beach, flower gardens, cliffs, and waterfall. The green tea fields add a different color and mood to the photo set, while the museum, cafe, restrooms, and optional tea purchases make it a comfortable final stop before the drive back to Jeju City. It also adds a recognizable Korean tea-culture story rather than ending the day with another flower garden.

items.label: Beach as Light Opener
items.detail: Hyeopjae as a low-intensity start lets the group settle before higher-attention bloom and geology sites.

items.label: Two Hydrangea Stops, Different Formats
items.detail: Hallim festival garden and Camellia tunnel give complementary compositions of the same flower — bookend morning and afternoon.

items.label: Tea to Close
items.detail: Osulloc closes calmly — culture and rest after the visual intensity of bloom and falls.

items.label: Hydrangea Season Late May - mid-July
items.detail: Hydrangea blooms appear from late May, peak mid-June, and fade by mid-July. Outside this period the bloom stops lose their visual draw.

items.label: Outside the Season, Choose SW-Small
items.detail: If your dates fall outside hydrangea season, our West & South year-round route covers the same region with year-round POIs (Hallasan, Osulloc, Aewol).

items.label: Camellia Hill is Not Camellia Season
items.detail: Despite the name, this tour visits Camellia Hill specifically for hydrangea — actual camellias bloom in winter.

items.label: 9 Hours Is Long
items.detail: Six diverse stops in 9 hours — pace is fuller than east-small. Build energy with steady hydration.

items.label: Some Stops Have Admission
items.detail: Hallim Park (15,000 KRW) and Camellia Hill (10,000 KRW) admissions are paid on-site, not bundled into the tour price. Budget ≈30,000 KRW per person across all admissions (Jusangjeolli 2,000 + Cheonjeyeon 2,500 round it out).

items.label: Heat Can Be Real
items.detail: June Jeju can hit 30°C+ at midday. Bring sun protection — most stops are outdoor.

staticQuestions.question: Is this good for first-time visitors to Jeju?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes, if you're visiting during hydrangea season (late May to mid-July, peak mid-June). Outside that period, our West & South year-round route covers the same region with year-round POIs.

staticQuestions.question: When exactly is hydrangea season?
staticQuestions.answer: Approximately late May to mid-July (peak mid-June), with peak typically in mid-June. We update the booking page with year-specific forecast in early May.

staticQuestions.question: What if it rains?
staticQuestions.answer: Light rain — we operate (hydrangea actually photographs beautifully in soft rain). Heavy rain — we offer free rescheduling within the season.

staticQuestions.question: Why two hydrangea stops?
staticQuestions.answer: Hallim Park is an open festival garden; Camellia Hill is a tunnel format. Same flower, complementary compositions.

staticQuestions.question: Is this suitable for children?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes, age 6+. Walking is moderate at most stops; hydrangea gardens are family-friendly.

staticQuestions.question: Is this ok for seniors?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes, mostly easy walking. Cheonjeyeon waterfall path has some stairs — can be skipped while group views Jusangjeolli.

staticQuestions.question: Where do you pick up?
staticQuestions.answer: Hotel lobby pickup within Jeju City is free. Pickup from Seogwipo, Aewol, or Seongsan adds 70,000 KRW due to extra driving distance.

staticQuestions.question: Is lunch included?
staticQuestions.answer: Lunch cost is separate. Expect 12,000-18,000 KRW per person.

staticQuestions.question: Are admission fees included?
staticQuestions.answer: Admissions are not bundled by default. Budget ≈30,000 KRW per person: Hallim Park 15,000, Camellia Hill 10,000, Jusangjeolli 2,000, Cheonjeyeon 2,500, Osulloc free.

staticQuestions.question: Is Osulloc included?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — museum entry is free. The tea house is separate (optional purchase).

staticQuestions.question: What's the difference vs our West & South year-round route?
staticQuestions.answer: Different POIs and seasonality. Our West & South year-round route is year-round and visits Hallasan 1100, Osulloc, and Aewol. This tour is summer-only and adds Hyeopjae, Hallim, Jusangjeolli, Cheonjeyeon, Camellia Hill while keeping Osulloc as the closing stop.

unesco_fit.reasoning: Verified — NO direct UNESCO World Heritage stops on this tour. Hallim Park caves are Korean National Monument (No. 236), NOT UNESCO. Jusangjeolli is Korean Natural Monument (No. 443), NOT UNESCO. Cheonjeyeon, Hyeopjae, Camellia Hill, Osulloc are non-UNESCO. Mechanical 1.0 was overstated. 0.45 reflects strong national-heritage anchors but no direct UNESCO.

walking_level.reasoning: Verified: Hyeopjae photo-stop (minimal), Hallim 1.5-3 km flat with cave ducking, Jusangjeolli 0.5 km boardwalk, Cheonjeyeon ~150 stairs to mid falls, Camellia Hill ~1.5 km flat, Osulloc 0.5 km. Total ~5 km mostly flat with one stair section. 0.50 calibrated.

scenic_level.reasoning: Verified: turquoise beach + 9-area botanical garden + 2 lava-tube caves + columnar basalt cliff + three-tier waterfall + camellia hydrangea tunnel + green tea fields. 7 distinct landscapes — among catalog's most diverse. 0.90 calibrated.

photo_level.reasoning: Verified: 6+ hero shots — Hyeopjae beach + Hallim caves/folk village + Jusangjeolli columns + Cheonjeyeon nymphs + Camellia hydrangea tunnel + Osulloc fields. Catalog-leading photo density. 0.95.

iconic_landmark_fit.reasoning: Verified: Hyeopjae Top 3 Jeju beach + Hallim Park 1.2M annual visitors + Jusangjeolli Natural Monument 443 + Cheonjeyeon (only 3-tier falls on Jeju) + Osulloc (Jeju's most famous tea brand). 5/6 internationally iconic. 0.85.

indoor_ratio.reasoning: CORRECTION using time-weighted method (Fix 1 methodology): 108 indoor / 405 total = 0.27 with lunch; 60/345 = 0.17 without. Catalog value 0.20 represents both. Mechanical 0.4 overstated.

weather_sensitivity.reasoning: VERIFIED 0.85 — hydrangea bloom timing weather-dependent; Hyeopjae + Jusangjeolli + Camellia Hill + Osulloc all primarily outdoor. Hallim caves provide partial weather refuge.

rain_fit.reasoning: Hallim caves (~600m total) + Osulloc indoor museum + restaurant lunch provide refuge. But majority of stops outdoor. 0.55 calibrated.

first_time_fit.reasoning: Verified: covers 7 distinct Jeju landscapes — among the most representative single-day southwest summaries. Season-specific to summer for hydrangea. 0.85.

value_for_money_fit.reasoning: Verified fees: Hyeopjae free + Hallim ₩11,000 + Jusangjeolli ₩2,000 + Cheonjeyeon ₩2,500 + Camellia Hill ₩9,000 + Osulloc free = ~₩24,500/adult. Higher than other Jeju tours due to multiple paid stops. 0.70.

young_kids_fit.reasoning: UPGRADED — Hallim Safari Bird Garden + caves + folk village + Hyeopjae Beach + Cheonjeyeon ravine. Strong family appeal. 0.85.

senior_fit.reasoning: UPGRADED — most stops senior-accessible (Hallim flat + Hyeopjae photo-stop + Osulloc flat); Cheonjeyeon stairs are the one challenge. 0.65.

stroller_fit.reasoning: UPGRADED — Hallim caves require ducking; otherwise mostly stroller-friendly. 0.65 calibrated for the cave caveat.

beach_fit.reasoning: UPGRADED — Hyeopjae IS the beach component (Top 3 Jeju). Photo-stop format limits beach time. 0.55.

culture_level.reasoning: Verified: Hallim Folk Village + Cheonjeyeon Seven Nymphs legend + Osulloc tea heritage. 3/6 cultural anchors. 0.55.

nature_fit.reasoning: Verified: beach + botanical garden + caves + columnar cliff + waterfall + flower park + tea fields. Catalog-leading nature density. 0.90.

geological_fit.reasoning: Verified: Hyeopjaegul + Ssangyonggul caves (lava+limestone) + Jusangjeolli columnar joints. 2 distinct geological landmarks. 0.85.

_extended_dimensions_documented_only.note: These dimensions (snow_fit, active_fit, geological_fit, experiential_fit) were assessed during the v17 verified-fact audit but are NOT yet part of the standard 68-key matching_profile. They are preserved here as documentation for future schema extension.
